Lead Applicant.Department: Children and Young People's Directorate Professional Background Allied Health Professional Detailed Professional Physiotherapist Background Professional Registration HCPC

APPLICATION DETAILS.Patient & Public Involvement:
I am structuring my strategy around Health Research Authority/INVOLVE guidance on how public
involvement strengthens ethical quality (relevance, acceptability, informed assent, participation,
communication). This is essential to support children with complex mobility/learning needs, whose
experiences are shaped by intersectionality and geodemographic disadvantage. Acceptability must
include children themselves, even when communication is complex. Through partnership working I will
deepen understanding of ethical issues specific to this community. My preparatory work has two
advisory groups: Children’s Participation Lab (ChiP, led by Gimeno) and the Willow Dene school
TRYcycle group. My physiotherapy experience grounds decisions in reality, and the pre doctoral award
provides protected time for meaningful co production ahead of a doctoral fellowship.
Lived experience has already shaped direction. A parent co authored a conference poster describing
cycling as ‘the most important health activity’ in her daughter’s life; a young PPI advisor reframed
language by describing physical activity as ‘moving with intention’; and a third young person proposed a
shift from ‘performance to participation’, raising the concept of ‘best interest’ decisions (for who?), and
asking how children with limited energy are enabled to choose where to spend it. What we will explore
(1): I will continue my partnership with the ChiP Lab, the advisory group for the NIHR Dystonia IMPACT
and Co-SMART trial. Meeting every second month provides a structured route to generate ideas and
widen perspectives across ages, mobility and communication profiles. I work one-day a week with a
Young Advisor employed as a lived experience contributor. His social media and entrepreneurial skills
expand team capability and support iterative feedback. We will use accessible materials and established
NIHR reimbursement routes to support consistent involvement. One member has reviewed this
submission, and a young person/parent will review the full doctoral application once drafted. (2)
TRYcycle: In parallel, I will facilitate structured touchpoints in special schools to bring learning back into
programme development. The focus is what matters to children during cycling and how this links to
assent. Through delivering the programme daily, organising a face-to-face workshop, and facilitating
peer advocacy cycle sessions, we will scope; what feels safe and enjoyable; how effort/comfort/pride are
noticed; how pacing, positioning and peers influence participation; when a child wants to stop; and what
outcomes look like beyond distance travelled/minutes cycled. Children and staff have already
highlighted practical enablers such as quieter spaces, predictable routines, and trusted supporters. The
advisory group will discuss multimodal routes of assent, explore signals of dissent, and consider school
context feasibility. To evidence progress, I will keep a simple ‘you said, we did’ log, an experience check
(clarity, comfort, choice), and meeting notes stating who was involved and what changes next.
My team strengthens this preparatory work. Dr Shepherd offers national expertise in research involving
people with impaired decision making capacity. Professor Smith brings internationally recognised
leadership in co production. Dr Gimeno the experience in research with children with complex needs.
Their input will help ensure rigorous methods and clear documentation of influence across both strands.

SUPPORT AND MENTORSHIP: Organisation OXLEAS NHS FOUNDATION TRUST Email Address faith.brooks1@nhs.net Role Practice Supervisor Description of Support My practice supervisor and line manager Faith Brooks is a Band 8b Provided Speech and Language Therapist (HCPC/RCSLT) with 25 years’ experience in paediatric early years communication and a dual clinical–operational leadership role at Oxleas. We meet weekly for a service lead huddle and formally for my supervision every six weeks. Supervision sessions are logged on the Trust learning platform, and we have an agreed ad hoc route for time critical issues. Faith’s dual vantage point is central to my clinical academic trajectory. She mentors my day to day strategic and operational workstreams, supports workload balance and protected research time, and provides a clear escalation pathway through service leadership if pressures arise. Her clinical expertise directly matches my predoctoral/doctoral programme, which will explore how developmental profiles and communication needs shape participation in physical activity and daily living among children with complex mobility and communication needs. Through supervision, we will be able to discuss the progress of my tangible outputs and identify any facilitators or barriers as they arise. Faith will be able to connect me to Speech and Language Therapy networks internally and externally to help build the multidisciplinary framework, embedding communication goals and the life context definition of assent as the programme develops. As an operational lead, she will feed emerging findings into directorate priorities, enabling timely translation into quality improvement and service pathways, while ensuring Oxleas’ host commitments are honoured through iterative feedback, check ins and formal progress reviews. This practice supervision complements academic supervision (methods and scholarly direction) by focusing on operational feasibility, workforce interfaces, and service adoption. Progress will be measured and reviewed quarterly ,giving NIHR a visible line of sight from supervision to service level impact. Full Name Dr Hortensia Gimeno Organisation Queen Mary University of London Email Address h.gimeno@qmul.ac.uk Role Primary Academic Supervisor Description of Support I am a Children’s Clinical Academic Occupational Therapist, Clinical Provided Reader in Child Health at Queen Mary University of London (QMUL) and Associate Director for Therapies Research at Barts Health. I currently hold a NIHR Advanced fellowship, and I exemplify the type of clinician the NIHR seeks to nurture and amplify. My career demonstrates that world-class, theory-driven research can be generated from within clinical practice, and that AHPs can lead interdisciplinary programmes delivering impact for patients, services, and the wider research ecosystem. Through establishing joint clinical academic posts, supporting progression from pre-doctoral to post- doctoral levels, and embedding AHPs within interdisciplinary research teams, I have contributed to a step-change in how clinical academic careers are realised in AHP research and have supported over 20 AHPs on pre-doctoral, doctoral, and post-doctoral fellowships. Since completing my PhD in 2019, I have developed an internationally competitive research portfolio despite highly constrained research- protected time. As such I am well versed on what the challenges are to develop a research career when there is little infrastructure around you or when working in naïve research environments. I have a national and international profile in leading research for childhood-onset complex movement disorders. My primary research programme addresses critical gaps in paediatric neurorehabilitation, particularly for children with rare conditions, intellectual disability, and childhood-onset movement disorders who are frequently excluded from research. I have led the development and evaluation of theory-driven, person-centred rehabilitation interventions targeting activity and participation outcomes meaningful to children and families, generating some of the first high-quality intervention evidence in this field (Neurology; Developmental Medicine & Child Neurology). Since September 2024, I have built and lead a children’s health research group comprising seven AHP researchers, methodologists, and co- researchers with lived experience, enabling inclusive research with underserved populations. This work has informed clinical pathways, contributed to international clinical practice guidelines, and expanded into policy through my role as co-applicant and work-package lead on an NIHR Policy Research Programme grant addressing workforce modelling for paediatric therapists supporting children and young people with special educational needs and disabilities (SEND). My movement disorder expertise, including cerebral palsy, current research program, and trajectory as a clinical academic occupational therapist expertly positions me to support Claire’s fellowship. I am deeply committed to increasing allied health professionals' capacity and capability for research, and Claire and my research interests to ensure children’s voices are heard, particularly those currently under- represented in research, align and are complimentary. Academic experience: I hold an NIHR Advanced Fellowship (2024-2028) to develop a research program for children and young people with movement disorders. My research, rooted in a solid theoretical and clinical foundation, aims to facilitate the optimal participation of children in daily life. Throughout my research career, I have demonstrated a dedication to understanding and addressing real-life problems. My experience, spanning over 28 years in the NHS, reflects my commitment to embedding research in clinical practice and utilising routinely collected data to enhance the evidence base. This aligns with the origin, aims and long-term objectives of Claire’s TRYcycle research plan. I have received over £7million in previous research funding through funders such as EU Horizon 2020, Rosetrees Trust, Barts Health Charity, and my previous NIHR Clinical Doctoral Fellowship. I am especially interested in individual trajectories and employ innovative research methodologies, such as single-case experimental design/n- of-1 trials, to measure intervention outcomes, particularly for populations underserved in research. Children with complex movement disorders are an underserved population in research and I am a passionate advocate of advancing research studies in this domain. This directly maps to Claire’s focus as a clinical academic and affords her a strong clinical bridge between health and the school environment in which she is situated. I am well positioned to support Claire in developing her own fellowship application, particularly as an experienced AHP researcher. Claire and I have been working closely on developing her research skills and I have provided learning and research hands-on experience for the past few years. More recently I have supported Claire closely throughout her NIHR awarded Pre-Application Support Fund (PASF) and she has an honorary appointment at QMUL with my group. During the pre-doctoral fellowship I will provide academic supervision and valuable advice on developing Claire’s clinical academic career, both during and post-fellowship. I will support in day-to-day aspects of the research, and I will meet Claire every other week for supervision, and monthly for joint sessions with Professor Brett, Claire’s secondary supervisor. Research Environment: Claire’s fellowship will be hosted at Oxleas NHS Trust with an honorary contract at QMUL Centre for Bone and Joint Health (the ChiP Lab). Claire will benefit from being embedded in this group with other clinical academic AHPs, such as Dr Lisa Newington (clinical academic senior lecturer hand therapist), Professor of Rehabilitation and first physiotherapist to be awarded a UKRI Future Leaders Fellowship Dr Katie Sheehan and other AHP PhD students. This group has strong links to the Pragmatic Clinical Trials Unit from QMUL. During this pre-doctoral fellowship, I will support Claire to develop collaborations she will have the opportunity to be involved in my program of research to enhance her skills and knowledge. Established Relationships: I have been working with Claire during her HEE internship, Pre-application support fund, and developing this pre- doctoral application. We have had regular discussions on the skills Claire needs to develop and refine her research questions and methods. I have already provided Claire with opportunities, such as nominating her to present at the National Posture and Mobility Group conference in 2022. I have met regularly with Claire, together with Professor Bret Smith (my secondary supervisor) to develop this pre-doctoral application. Full Name Professor Brett Smith Organisation DURHAM UNIVERSITY Email Address brett.smith@durham.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Brett Smith is Professor of Disability and Physical Activity at Provided Durham University. His research expertise blends disability studies, public health and qualitative methodology, with a sustained record of co‑produced programmes that move from evidence to policy and practice. Brett is an author of the first UK Chief Medical Officers’ Physical Activity Guidelines for disabled children (2022), which was co‑produced with disabled children, families and practitioners. His work on the government guidelines is foundational to my research. Academic Studentships and Experience: Brett has supervised over 50 MSc students and 19 PhD students to completion and externally examined 41 PhDs internationally. He is a leading authority in qualitative methodology, founding the International Society of Qualitative Research in Sport and Exercise and co‑editing the Routledge Handbook of Qualitative Research in Sport and Exercise. Current Research and Alignment with My Work: Brett leads Moving Social Work, a multi‑year, co‑produced research and implementation programme with Disability Rights UK and Sport England (phase two funded at ~£1m) that designs, tests and scales resources embedding physical‑activity advocacy into social work education and practice through staged co‑design and national rollout. This programme has also produced peer‑reviewed guidance on co‑production in practice (typology, working principles, quality criteria), which I will translate directly into my consent‑enablement work. National/International Leadership: Brett’s guideline work exemplifies policy‑level co‑production, from evidence review to co‑produced infographic and animation with disabled children and families, demonstrating how participatory design improves message relevance and sector adoption. He co‑leads Durham’s Narrative Practices Lab (Wellcome Discovery Research Platform), using narrative and lived experience to co‑create health and social‑care tools. Research Environment and Support: Durham University offers a strategic, research‑intensive environment spanning 27 academic departments and over 40 research institutes, with Brett’s affiliations including the Durham Research Methods Centre and the Wellcome‑funded Narrative Practices Lab. This affords me access to transdisciplinary methods, policy partners and knowledge‑mobilisation pathways across health, education and social care. Bespoke support Plan: As my secondary supervisor, Brett will meet with me monthly (virtual) and host an in‑person placement with his team. Brett’s supervision will ensure that the programme is built on co production as a normative stance, not a technique, sharing power, jointly owning decisions, and valuing lived and professional knowledge as equal throughout the full research cycle. He will help me develop the ground rules that operationalise these principles (joint decision rights, reciprocal benefit, continuous dialogue, relationship based working). I will be supported to develop assent principles as an ongoing process shaped by relationships and context, ensuring inclusive voice, fair recognition, and ethical knowledge generation. This stewardship ensures that co production underpins, guides, and shapes the foundations of all aspects of the programme going forward. This ranges from who decides what counts as assent, to how findings are owned, authored, and implemented.
SUMMARY BUDGET: This Fellowship is the necessary bridge to a high quality doctorate. My previous training and MRes have strengthened my trajectory, but I still require focused development in: ethics and governance for research with children with complex needs; inclusive, co produced methods for assent/shared decision making; applied designs for complex studies; and guideline translation and implementation. Activities will map to the Vitae researcher framework to target competencies and evidence progress. Type of cost: Salary back fill £46,088.39 40% FTE, 15mth programme. Divided in to cost per month = £3107.90 April 2026– November 2027= 8 x £3107.90= £24,863.20 TOTAL YEAR 1 £21,225.19 + YEAR 2 £24,863.20= £46.088.59. The fellowship will provide the protected time to close remaining skills gaps and plan a competitive doctoral application. It will develop self management through practical experience balancing clinical and academic demands, and deliver tangible outputs: consolidation of first author publications and expand work with people and communities. Type of cost: Bespoke masters level training £1820 Co-producing and co-designing health & social care interventions Level 7 | King's College London My context demands participatory methods that include non verbal children. This programme provides mentored, advanced skills in co design and practical use of the Medical Research Council/NIHR complex interventions framework. Travel costs: Train London to Newcastle single fare (£50); Train Newcastle to Durham single fare (£5); Train Durham to London (£50): Accommodation, 1 night stay between sites £140. Type of cost: Conference (£500) Attend the European Academy of Childhood Disability (EACD) 2027 conference, held in Greece. This is the most relevant international forum for my field. The NIHR contribution will cover the registration fee; travel and accommodation will be funded through my employer’s CPD budget, ensuring full resourcing of the trip. Attendance will enable me to present my work, develop clinical and research skills, and build international collaborations with childhood disability experts, supported by Dr Gimeno.Type of cost Working with People and Communities (£500): Co produced engagement with children/young people and partners to shape my doctoral proposal.
